# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What's a movie that has no right to be as good as it is

**Score:** 183
**Body:** Am currently watching The 13th Warrior. I have watched it several times in the past, but not in several years. The concept is ridiculous but it's such a good movie. What are some others 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Galaxy Quest might be the perfect movie.
  - “Look! I have one job on this lousy ship, it's stupid, but I'm gonna do it! Okay?!"
2. Ridiculous concept, but surprisingly good: Tremors 
  - I’d say Tremors is an absolute classic at this point.
3. Drop dead gorgeous
4. Tucker and Dale vs. Evil
5. This movie gets glossed over A LOT, but Brother Bear (2003). 
6. The Lego Movie
7. Dnd honor among thieves@
8. I know everybody else loves it too but I got to say I was so surprised when I sat down for a pirate movie with my kids. 
Pirates of the Caribbean the original.

## 2. Suggest me a 10/10 movie where no one dies

**Score:** 111
**Body:** Majority of the great movies that I've seen rely on someone's death as a plot point or for driving the climax, and I'm tired of that. Suggest me an utterly beautiful/kickass/amazing movie that doesn't rely on death, where the writing/screenplay itself is great enough to make it a 10/10 film. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Martian. One of my favourite films, there's not even an antagonist. And spoiler alert, no deaths.
2. Ocean’s Eleven
  - My fav of all time watched over a 100 times.
3. Spirited Away
4. Groundhog Day
  - One person dies!
  - Not for lack of trying
5. Can people already be dead or come back to life? 


Muppet Christmas Carol 
6. Dazed and Confused
7. Spotlight

When Harry Met Sally

Victor/Victoria

My Cousin Vinny (there is an off screen murder of someone we meet for 10 seconds, and mention of the death penalty taking place, but it’s such a lighthearted, funny comedy, I think you’d be okay)

## 3. First date cancelled last minute. Need a movie to watch

**Score:** 33
**Body:** He texted 30 minutes before to just say he couldn’t make it. No reason why and I don’t care to ask/know. I’ve cast him to land of lost men. 46F and seeking a movie to fit this situation! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He’s just not that into you
2. **Monster (2003)**
3. send help
4. Maybe weird but Goodfellas or Casino always makes me feel better 😂
  - Casino! Nothing like watching Sharon Stone own the screen!
5. Gone Girl
6. **THE SEVENTH SEAL (1957)**
7. Jurassic Park. “God creates dinosaurs. God destroys dinosaurs. God creates man. Man destroys God. Man creates dinosaurs.” “Dinosaurs eat man. Woman inherits the earth.”
8. To balance things out, you next need to find a last date who cancels first minute.
9. [**Promising Young Woman**](https://www.google.com/search?q=Promising+Young+Woman&rlz=1C1CHBF_enUS982US982&oq=female+revenge+movies&gs_lcrp=EgZjaHJvbWUqBwgAEAAYgAQyBwgAEAAYgAQyBggBEEUYOTIHCAIQABiABDIHCAMQABiABDIHCAQQABiABDIHCAUQABiABDIHCAYQABiABDIHCAcQABiABDIHCAgQABiABDIHCAkQABjvBdIBCTExNDU3ajBqOagCALACAfEFQjosyzbgeEw&sourceid=chrome&ie=UTF-8&ved=2ahUKEwif0-7e7uuSAxUNnSYFHb6UF-4QgK4QegYIAQgHEAE) **(2020)**

## 4. Suggest me a 80s/90s movie with similar vibes to Don't Tell Mom the Babysitter's Dead

**Score:** 30
**Body:** I'm looking for silly totally 90s/80s movies that are silly and fun and weird like this one. Similar in vibe or anything with a crazy premise that would only be made in that time!

Thanks!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Adventures in babysitting.Mr Mom. The burbs.
2. Adventures in Babysitting
3. Weekend at Bernie's
4. You want a SILLY movie? 

Drop dead Fred. Filled to the brim with quotable lines!
5. So I married an axe murderer
6. I agree with Adventures in Babysitting and Weekend at Bernies.

Also might like:

Ferris Bueller's Day Off

License to Drive

Airheads

Better Off Dead

Home Alone

Uncle Buck
7. Son in Law (1993)
8. Throw Momma from the Train (1987)
9. Overboard maybe? It's def silly!
10. Just One of the Guys (1986) : Very similar imposter kind of movie, but with a gender twist!

## 5. The Limey (1999)

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Soderbergh’s The Limey (1999) is ostensibly a standard, hard-boiled thriller, but it's actually a Trojan horse. At its core, the film is a haunting meditation on regret; a version of regret that can turn a person into a phantom seeking vengeance in order to feel tethered to the world.

Terence Stamp plays Wilson, a dangerous British ex-con who travels to glitzy Los Angeles to investigate his daughter's suspicious death. He’s out for blood, but as he clashes with the vapid, wealthy L.A. elite, it becomes devastatingly clear that the real enemy he's fighting is his own past. 

Runtime: 89 minutes 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It was awesome how they got footage from an old Terrance Stamp movie to make flashback scenes


  - Meta in all the good and creative ways
2. Excellent flic! I've watched it three times
  - TS : *What're we standing on?*

LG : *Faith.*

....

LG : *You could see the sea if you could see it.*

....

Stellar dialogue throughout.
3. I’ve just seen it, I recommend! Good film and great editing.
4. Going in, I had a very clear idea of what I was going to get and I have seldom been so happy to be wrong. It’s just magnificent.
5. RIP to Nicky Katt — who in that moves between “hilarious” and “chilling” like he’s flipping a switch. Wish that dude had done more
  - > RIP to Nicky Katt

Ah, man.  I didn't know.  Poor guy.
6. Bill Duke is amazing in everything
  - "There is one thing I don't understand..."

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Remembering Alan Rickman on what would’ve been his 80th birthday. Do you have a favorite character he played?

**Score:** 559
**Body:** I’ll start. Hans Gruber in Die Hard. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "By Grabthar's Hammer, by the Suns of Warvan, you shall be avenged."
  - "By Grabthar's Hammer, what a savings"
2. Hans Gruber.

The one, the only.
  - Now I have a machine gun. Ho…Ho………Ho
3. The Sherrif of Nottingham
  - And call off Christmas!

You 10:00. You, 10:30, bring a friend.

THAT’S my wife…Crone!

So many memorable lines. He was amazing in that role.

4. The Metatron.

Rest easy sir
5. Marvin - Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy

Dr. Alfred Blalock - Something the Lord Made
6. Hands down the Sheriff of Nottingham in Robin Hood Prince of Thieves
7. So many great answers from such a great career, but I think I love him being able to play Col. Brandon in Sense and Sensibility the most.  It was cool seeing him play a romantic lead in his own quiet way.

## 2. Do you agree that Christian Bale has great range?

**Score:** 532
**Body:** I’ve heard a lot of people say Christian has a lot of range. Tbh I just dont see it. His range isn’t that good imo compared to Gary Oldman’s or Ralph Fienness. 

Do you agree? What movies should I watch with him? 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Karma farming?
  - 100% this - pure rage bait
2. You dont see it?

What Bale movies have you seen?
  - The bathman
    -   
Bathman 😀

https://preview.redd.it/91zd7nluevkg1.jpeg?width=1024&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=645bc79d73f4be0e7f7cae61cf67a59a9a75dba0


      - The shower is his greatest challenge
3. Bro has already covered many genres
4. ![gif](giphy|YtvCIwqNJhUmA)
5. Yes. He's the greatest living actor and I've been dying on that hill for almost 10 years now.
  - He’s in S tier for me, with Gary Oldman, DDL, and willem dafoe

## 3. Heartbreakingly beautiful

**Score:** 431
**Body:** Set in modern-day Tokyo, RENTAL FAMILY follows an American actor (Brendan Fraser) who struggles to find purpose until he lands an unusual gig: working for a Japanese "rental family" agency, playing stand-in roles for strangers. As he immerses himself in his clients’ worlds, he begins to form genuine bonds that blur the lines between performance and reality. Confronting the moral complexities of his work, he rediscovers purpose, belonging, and the quiet beauty of human connection.

The performances are great, starting with Brendan Fraser but everyone truely deliverd. The screenplay is deep, thoughtful and very real. The cinematography is lush. From a technical stand point this movie delivers and from a thematic and emotional stand point even more so. You really feel the characters loneliness and desperate need for connection. So much loneliness that hey hire actors to play the missing parts in their lives. A triumph overall.

8/10


### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. My wife REALLY struggled with the concept and felt like that daughter would be scarred for life. I have a hard time disagreeing. If you can hand wave that though then it is indeed extremely heartwarming.
  - Who amongst us wouldn't be OK with a random man meeting up with their pre-teen daughter who they've texted and held hands with?
  - Yes agreed! I generally enjoyed the move but definitely had to suspend belief about the relationship with the little girl, especially since the mom let him take her out all the time and didn’t even know him, and then the daughter overall being ok with it!
2. Film got really dark when it showed the twist with the boss
3. My wife and I loved this movie. Seemed like a realistic take on loneliness in Japan.
4. Great movie. Had zero expectations & found myself laughing & with a tear in the eye. Can't ask much more than that from a movie. BF is I believe between 6 foot 2 1/2 and 3 1/2. He looked like he was 10 feet tall. He is a very sympathetic actor. Would be interesting to see him in roles where he's not the nice or put-upon guy. Let's see him as a complete shit.
  - Doom Patrol isnt a movie, but you may enjoy
5. I still can’t help but think of the Conan episode where he rents a family in Japan. Adding this one to my watchlist
6. We loved it.
7. It is very sweet and an easy watch and it reminded me very much of the movie Perfect Days.. similar tone.. and everybody in it was very good especially the young girl.

## 4. Camp Nowhere (1994) and Heavyweights (1995)

**Score:** 380
**Body:** Turned today into a back-to-back summer camp movie day

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. On The Waterfront is a Masterpiece

**Score:** 53
**Body:** Rewatching On The Waterfront, I really forgot how great the film is.

This truly is one of Marlon Brando’s greatest film role and possibly the pinnacle of his early career. Brando just owns every scene as Terry Molloy, a man who is conflicted on whether or not to testify against Johnny Friendly, a mob boss who uses him to lure Joey Doyle onto a rooftop and into his death.

The cast, consisting of Karl Malden as Father Barry, Lee J. Cobb as Johnny Friendly, Rod Steiger as Charley Malloy, and Eva Marie Saint as Edie Doyle are just brilliant and I considered it one of the best casts ever assembled. With Malden, Cobb, and Steiger’s character, these 3 are essentially in Terry’s Arc and his conflictions on what to do. Father Barry being the one who wants Terry to do the right thing, and talking sense into him at his most dangerous. Johnny Friendly, who wants Terry to not testify and rat them out, and keep his mouth shut 

Then there is Charley Malloy, his brother. With Father Barry being the side of good and Johnny Friendly being the side of bad, Charley is in the middle. While he worked with Friendly, he still cares for his brother and it all culminates in the cab drive scene, in which Charley is ordered by Friendly to keep Terry quiet otherwise he will be killed. When Charley pulls a gun on Terry to make him take the job  looks hurt and Charley feels shame. Then they both reminiscing Terry's boxing days and how Terry stated that Charley took his boxing away over a bet. So Charley, in his last act, give Terry a chance, gives him his gun and car and makes him leave the cab. It was Charley’s act of mercy and ultimately his death, is what compels Terry to Testify.

We also have Edie Doyle, the sister of Joey Doyle who was killed and wants justice for his brother. Terry becomes acquaintance with her and becomes smitten to her, and eventually develops feelings for her. Because of these feelings, and after a talk with Father Barry, he reveals his role of Joey’s death to Edie, who is shock and breaks with with him, but after Terry leaves the Cab, he goes to Edie’s apartment and they reconcile, because both realize they love each other.

Honestly, this film is just great, and this is Elia Kazan’s magnum opus, who is also on his A Game with this film. I know this film was made as a response to Kazan testifying to the HUAC, but honestly, I don't care, On The Waterfront truly is a great film. 



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Strange take on his performance and I suspect it’s due to a lack of context. This is really the performance that demonstrated a new type of acting which the following generation all pointed to.

He was a pathetic character and assuming that a “bum” wouldn’t approach a woman is thinking in absolutes.

The climatic scene with his brother was not just about his regret, but the betrayal of trust. That was fairly obvious within the dialogue.

Brando was doing some amazing work that was not a part of the tool box of actors of that period. It wasn’t really in his delivery, but what he did with his body, his eyes, his posture, movement, and how he spaced out his responses to other actors. The scene in the bar with Edie is absolutely stunning.
  - Agree with all of this.


The famous glove interaction. People talk a lot, wrongly imho, about The Method with Brando but his real skill for me was in his naturalism. He fusses and fidgets. Picks at things. His movement was unparalleled and he seemed to have natural instincts when moving within a frame. Not showy at all but you can't take your eyes off him. Compelling in the most mundane of settings. 


The older generation were almost all trained dancers (Cagney was a helluva hoofer) and this gave them a grace on screen that many of today's actors don't have. You can see the dancer in Walken in all of his performances. He knows how to move.


I genuinely miss the refined art and composition of old Hollywood.
2. Great performances, but evil purpose with the anti-union angle. 

Not the first time a skillfully made film was evil.  Birth of a nation…leni…

Orson Welles called the film “a celebration of the informer”…of the rat

https://youtu.be/Z6DC4AjTG2M
  - Came here to post this, screw Elia Kazan. 

Elia Kazan was a rat and a traitor of the highest order.  He was responsible for ruining the lives of countless American artists, and his rehabilitation stands among the great moments of neglect amongst film people.
  - I do hate how the film is basically A03 fan fiction justifying shitty actions, but I will admit it is extraordinary filmmaking. Orson Welles takes the words out of my mouth, but with far more passion.

## 2. Was rewatching Shame (2011) and was wondering about that final argument between Brandon and Sissy, while sitting on the couch and watching tv; was anyone else intrigued by why the scene was shot that way? Meaning, from the back?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** I don't why but the entire vibe about that scene....I'm not able to put my finger on what exactly it is but.....there's just this really intense vibe about that entire scene (particularly from Brandon). 

And I know most of ya'll will chalk this upto Brandon being frustrated with Sissy and that intense frustration finally boiling over onscreen but.....that's not what I'm talking about. 

And yes, I've heard interpretations of the choice of cartoon playing on screen (or the fact that they're even watching cartoons) but again, please read my title; that's not what I'm referring to. 

I'm really struggling to put this in words but......I think it was deliberately shot from the back, with their faces that up-close to one another, with the camera lingering on their faces up close, unmoving. 

Usually whenever two characters are talking on screen, the camera will switch between the faces. But that's not the case here. 

And I'm not exactly sure what the intent was with that choice of direction but there's just this really intense vibe that I'm picking off of the scene and particularly from Brandon, that I'm not able to put my finger on what it is. 

And I know he already is kinda intense around his sister throughout the movie, but something about the way this scene was shot, made it stand out. 

Did anybody else feel that? Or sense that? 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. That POV is both removed and intimate. It lets us observe them without being in their faces, seeing what the other person is seeing. It takes us out of their conversation and allows us to just observe them. They have sat on a couch watching TV together many times in their lives probably, especially when they were children living in what must have been a very fucked up home. Being behind them, almost looming over them, kind of puts us in the position of all the horrible shit that has always loomed over them as they try to just be siblings and do something normal like watch TV together. It kind of reminds me of the cartoons playing in the background of Skinamarink, which also adds to the sadness of watching little kids trying to continue being little kids in a house where they are in danger and have no control over what happens to them.
2. To me it puts the viewer in their perspective. We’re sat behind them, almost with them, at the sofa with the cartoons playing in from of us. 

The image of an unfolding argument happening to the background of children’s cartoons is an image that reflects countless situations they would have experienced during their childhood in what must’ve been a very abusive household. In a way it’s a window into their past experiences now reflected in their adulthood. 

It’s also a very evocative image - I can certainly relate to the childhood experience of watching cartoons while arguments flame in the background and I think most people can also. In that way it’s a way of immersing us into the scene.
3. Quick AI explanation:

That final argument scene in Shame (2011) between Brandon (Michael Fassbender) and Sissy (Carey Mulligan) on the couch is indeed one of the most intensely charged moments in the film, and the direction to shoot it from behind—unbroken for over six minutes—is intentional and deeply unsettling for several reasons. [1]  
Here is an analysis of why that scene has such a heavy, intimate, and uncomfortable vibe, beyond simple frustration: 

• Forced Intimacy and Voyeurism: By placing the camera behind them, Steve McQueen forces the audience into the role of a silent witness inside a very private, claustrophobic space. We are not watching a traditional dramatic "fight" where characters yell at each other's faces; we are observing a deeply private, almost voyeuristic moment of dysfunction. 

• Physicalizing the "Bad Place": Sissy notes they come from a "bad place". The proximity of their heads, almost whispering their toxic, deeply personal accusations while ignoring the cartoon on the screen, emphasizes a shared, damaged history. It feels less like a fight between siblings and more like two people sharing a morbid, incestuous emotional bond, where only they can understand each other's pain. 

• Intense Emotional Repression: The shot forces us to focus on the nuance of their posture and hair rather than their facial expressions. For Brandon, who spends the entire movie trying to keep his emotions and addictions completely detached, this moment of being forced to connect emotionally with his sister—without the distraction of sex—is a source of immense internal torment. 

• The "Hugging Porcupines" Scenario: The scene feels like two people who need each other but hurt each other whenever they are close. The rear angle highlights this, showing them "together" but looking away, trapped in the same frame. 

• Unbroken Take: The fact that it is a 6-minute, 15-second unbroken take means the tension never breaks. The actors are allowed to build a genuine, uncomfortable atmosphere that feels entirely naturalistic rather than staged. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]  

In summary, the scene is shot that way to show that this is not just a fight, but a profound, isolating, and almost suffocating moment of trauma coming to the surface, where both characters are at their most vulnerable yet cannot help but be cruel to each other. 

AI responses may include mistakes.

[1] https://x.com/FilmFreeway/status/1166422528765698048
[2] https://movies.stackexchange.com/questions/72557/what-is-the-bad-place-the-protagonists-come-from-in-shame
[3] https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/9qdj8n/what_was_the_movie_shame_trying_to_say_about_the/
[4] https://elladonaldwriter.wordpress.com/2015/10/15/shame-2011/
[5] https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/9qdj8n/what_was_the_movie_shame_trying_to_say_about_the/
  - Fuck off lol
  - If he wanted AI he would have asked AI bro
    - You can't imagine my relief when I saw that comment was downvoted. Are we really gonna give everything to machines?
  - You're alive, write your own thoughts.
    - Well, they’re using AI, unlikely there’s many thoughts bouncing around in there
  - Fuck AI
  - Honestly this wasn’t a bad answer. I don’t like AI for discussions but the knee jerk dismissal isn’t right either.

## 3. Marty Supreme connected to Ravenous?

**Score:** 13
**Body:** Ok, so last night I watched Marty Supreme, and a very subtle connection to the film Ravenous has been driving me a little wild, and after some digging I’ve come up with nothing. Here’s the thing:

Shortly after Marty is defeated by the player from Japan, a black and white news reel plays, and about halfway through (after Endo tinkers with a machine) there’s a melody that plays in the background that sounds like standard military affair. But my brain immediately recognized the melody, and for a few moments I was fumbling to figure out where I had heard it before.

Then it clicked. The OST of the 1999 film Ravenous (which is amazing) has a track called “Boyd’s Journey” - and in that song, at the 1:48 mark, is that same exact melody played a little slower on trumpet, and I don’t think it’s a coincidence.

But I’ve not found a song to that melody that perhaps both films referenced separately. I’m very curious about this very minor detail, but wanted to throw it out here in case anyone else noticed this, or can identify the song?

Anyway, enjoyed the film. The music was very interesting how how anachronistic it was for the needle drops. But Daniel Lopatin’s score really evoked Wendy Carlos for me in a lot of ways, and was very pleasantly surprised by what it added to the film.

Anyway, thanks for reading!

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. First of all: Ravenous is an incredible soundtrack. I still haven’t seen the actual movie!

To remind myself of what Boyd’s Journey sounds like, I loaded it up on [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mfXxEUAUUqA). The musical passage you mention sounds super familiar… almost like a hymn or an old standard.

There it is in the comments: “Around 1:50 [Hail Columbia](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hKWIOmqdZP8) starts playing in the background, one of the first unofficial American anthems.”
  - You’ve got to watch it! It’s  100000000000000000x better than Marty Supreme, that’s for goddamn sure.
  - Holy hog! You found it! The context of the song does make sense for both films as well. One mocking an American defeat in an up and coming sport, the other parodying vampiric American colonialism. 

Thank you!

Also, you must see Ravenous. It’s very, very good.
2. No idea if it's connected to the music, but insofar as Ravenous is a movie about eating people, it may be of interest to note that [an early draft of Marty Supreme had a vampiric ending](https://variety.com/2026/film/news/marty-supreme-original-vampire-ending-1236631757/).
  - Kevin O’Leary *would* want to be an immortal bloodsucking parasite.
    - I do believe he was cast in the role because he wouldn't need to act at all.
3. The sci-fi film Men in Black 3 and the sci-fi anime Gantz share several plot devices, some crucial dialogue, and the backstory to one, immigration, is the main thematic element to the other. That said I would still say they have nothing to do with each other. Even if films or media share different ideas or maybe if they were even borrowed they still might just stand alone.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Good Will Hunting: The Power of Vulnerability, Overcoming Fear, and the Avoidant Mind

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Good Will Hunting was always my favorite film, and I recently broke down why *Good Will Hunting* remains one of the best portrayals of trauma and emotional defense mechanisms in cinema.

Will Hunting uses his intellect as a fortress. He relies on sarcasm and "book knowledge" to keep people at a distance. He rejects others before they have a chance to reject him. This is most visible in his early therapy sessions where he treats the process like a game to be won rather than a chance to grow.

Main points of my analysis:

* Intellectual Armor: How Will’s genius is actually a security system designed to keep him safe from pain.
* Two Father Figures: The difference between Professor Lambeau, who values Will for his potential, and Sean Maguire, who offers unconditional presence.
* The Courage to Leave: Why Chuckie’s famous "ten seconds" speech is the ultimate act of friendship. It proves that real loyalty is about wanting your friend to find a better life, even if it means they leave you behind.

The film's visual journey is also important. It begins with a unique kaleidoscope effect during the opening credits. This suggests that Will is a complicated person made of many pieces that the viewer must slowly put together. The story starts with Will alone in a dark, cramped room and ends with him driving on an open highway. This represents his move from a safe but stagnant life to a free and uncertain future.

I wrote a more detailed breakdown of these psychological layers. If you are interested in a deeper look at Will’s journey and his breakthrough with Sean, you can read the full article here: [**https://www.playforthoughts.com/blog/good-will-hunting**](https://www.playforthoughts.com/blog/good-will-hunting)
